data.title: 2 High-Yield Dividend Aristocrats That Are On Sale
data.description:
Many things have changed in the markets since the President Trump took the Office. Most of the changes have a negative connotation in the context of asset allocation and shareholder returns.

For example:

  • Most of the equities are down (both growth and value).
  • Fixed income has declined as well.
  • The uncertainty levels are very high, which have forced some investor groups to completely pull out the capital from the markets (e.g., those that are about to retire and rely on harvesting portfolio gains).
  • The inflation problems are likely to return, but it is difficult to predict whether these effects will be transitory or with some long-lasting consequences.
  • The overall growth will most probably experience real pressures, which could potentially trigger elevated bankruptcies, especially in the weaker segments of the economy.

The only positive I see from all of this is that the valuations have come down a bit that has, among other things, lifted up the yields for both risk-on and risk-off assets.

In my opinion, the question is how we can capitalize on this positive nuance that has emerged as a consequence of the recent (and still to some extent ongoing) shock-therapy.

My approach is simple: remain biased towards non-cyclical businesses that can handle both inflation and weaker growth problems in a manner that does not impair their underlying value and the ability to keep distributing attractive dividends.

Also, an important disclosure here is that my investing approach is price-agnostic - i.e., it really does not matter for me how the prices evolve as long as the dividends are stable and growing at a rate that exceeds the inflation.

Here are two relatively high yield dividend aristocrats that I see being on sale.

#1 - Enterprise Products Partners, 7%

Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (NYSE:EPD) is, in my view, a true definition of durable income producing asset. It has distributed cash to its unitholders for 26 years in a row, recording no reductions in the process.

The recent market sell-off has also affected EPD, as the stock has lost around 9% over the past couple of weeks. As a result of this, we have the option to lock in EPD at a relatively decent yield of 7%.

Now, what are the key reasons why EPD has lost so much from its market cap.

There are two.

First, the consequences from the broader market dynamics that have led to capital outflows from equity-like products.

Second, and this is more specific, the energy prices have plunged, which, in turn, opens up many theoretical risks to a business that operates in the oil and/or nat gas space.

For instance, lower energy prices lead to reduced production and new investment amounts. If the economy really contracts, then the chances are very high that we will see a drop in demand for the energy.

For the upstream businesses it is, obviously, a huge shock.

For the midstream segment the situation is not that bad as irrespective of prices, new investments and changes in the production volumes, there will always be a need to move around the carbon molecules (both domestically and internationally into the export markets).

However, I would say that EPD is much better positioned than the rest of its midstream peers.

I will give you 5 arguments.

First, about 90% of EPD's income comes from fee-based (take or pay) contracts that provide downside protection on both the volume and price front. Plus, these contracts in most cases come with embedded CPI revenue escalators, which can advance the cash flows even if the volume/price floor is met.

Second, EPD has, arguably, one of the best balance sheets in the game. It has an upper investment grade capital structure with a weighted average debt yield of 4.7%. The latter aspect is quite important as it indicates an immaterial gap between the current IG financing yields and what has been carried by EPD (i.e., the cash flow pressures from debt refinancings are insignificant).

Third, EPD's distribution coverage is 1.7x, which gives plenty of margin of safety to absorb financial shocks without impairing the distribution durability/sustainability factor. Plus, the surplus undistributed cash flow can be nicely channeled into either new accretive CapEx projects or opportunistic buybacks without involving incremental debt.

Fourth, EPD plays across almost the whole midstream value chain. It has pipes, storage facilities, fractionation and processing. It introduces a decent lever of diversification, which comes in handy during times like these.

Fifth, I think that it is worth to appreciate the historical cash flow track record as a separate factor, which drives the investment case here. In my view, the chart below captures the entire essence of EPD very well. It just shows how durable the business model is. Even in times of totally collapsing and for a short period negative oil prices, EPD has managed to deliver stable cash flows.

In a nutshell, EPD is an easy choice for investors, who seek sustainable and above average current income streams.

#2 - Realty Income Corporation, 5.7%

Realty Income Corporation (NYSE:O) is another high-quality dividend aristocrat gem that has gone through a huge roller coaster ride since the long-term yield started to go in the unfavorable direction (from around October 2024). The past month and weeks have not been the greatest either, but, currently, the stock price has recovered from the dip. Yet, the stock is down by ~8% starting from October, last year.

In terms of the yield, what we, currently, can get is almost the highest level the O has provided over the past 10-year period. It is because the share price has remained somewhat stagnant, while the business has nicely registered a ~4% dividend 10-year CAGR.

Many of my investors have probably heard me (or read my articles) many times plotting a bull case on O. So, I will quickly outline the most important fundamental elements, which, in my view, form a compelling dividend case. Then I will touch upon an interesting nuance/advantage, which has not been discussed too much in the context of O's bull case.

So, the fundamentals that make O a "sleep-well-at-night" dividend investment are:

  • Upper investment grade balance sheet, which for REITs is more important than for MLPs (e.g., EPD). This is because REITs tend to assume more leverage, when doing M&As (i.e., buying properties). So, the notion of having an upper investment grade credit rating allows O to both avoid mitigate the unfavorable refinancing risks and be more competitive when buying growth properties.
  • Strongly diversified business model. While it is primarily a net-lease retail REIT, a tangible chunk of leases stems from data centers, gaming and industrial segments, which are, inherently, more defensive than conventional retail. Plus, we have to keep in mind that O is one of the largest REITs, which helps mitigate single-tenant concentration risk. Finally, O invests heavily in the UK and Europe that further enhances the diversification aspect.
  • O retains about 25% of its FFO generation in its books that just as in the case of EPD contributes to a solid margin of safety and a debt-free growth potential. Speaking of the FFO and the cash flows, a weighted average lease term of ~10 years with ~2% annual lease escalators ensure a stable growth factor that can come in handy when the M&A opportunities are put on pause.

Now, the aspect, which is rather unique about O (in a positive sense) is its exposure to the non-US markets. Here is a historical statistic showing how massively O has deployed capital into the EU markets. Plus, in the past couple of years, O has started to invest in the UK market as well. So, the total lease share that stems from non-US markets is a bit less than 20%.

There are a couple of positives from this, like the benefit of weakening USD versus GBP and EUR (i.e., the GBP and EUR cash flows converted to USD have now much bigger impact than before the tariff-saga). Furthermore, O can enjoy the benefit of tighter credit spread and lower all-in yields in the EU IG capital markets space.

The bottom line

Synchronized market drawdowns create opportunities for investors to buy high-quality at cheaper prices. For income investors, these cheaper prices mean higher entry yields, which is a huge benefit.

Granted, we have to be careful of not falling into value traps. We have to give some respect to the market and appreciate that it is still a future cash flow discounting machine that factors in new risks and opportunities.

However, as long the underlying business fundamentals are robust enough, relatively non-cyclical, and there is no aggressive risk taking from the management's side, we can really capitalize on these kinds of opportunities.

In this context, EPD and O are great picks that patient income investors can choose now to complement their current income streams.

data.title: It Will Be A Seesaw Moving Forward
data.description:
Yesterday was a snooze compared to what investors have had to endure over the past two weeks since the reciprocal tariff program was announced on “liberation day.” I think the relative calm has come because the stock, bond, and currency markets have been temporarily pacified by the numerous pivots, pauses, and exceptions to a tariff policy that would have raised the average levy on US imports from approximately 2.5% at the start of this year to a staggering 27%. At that rate, a deep consumer-led recession would be a foregone conclusion, which is why the S&P 500 narrowly dodged a bear market decline.

The index has now rallied more than 11% from its correction low of 4835, but further progress will depend on what I view as a seesaw with trade policy on one end and stock, bond, and currency markets on the other. If tariffs come down, risk asset prices will go up. If tariffs remain in place, stocks will drift lower, bond prices will decline and yields will rise, while the dollar will continue to weaken. The president has indicated he won’t change his mind on tariffs, but the multiple modifications to his policy suggest otherwise. For now, it looks like a decline to 4800 on the S&P 500 or an increase in the 10-year yield above 4.5% are the catalysts for another pivot, but eventually, it will be a deterioration in the high frequency economic data that forces him to pivot again.

Last week I surmised that we had 30 days to fix this because the longer our economy is stuck with the status quo of uncertainty, the more likely abysmal consumer and investor sentiment bleeds into the real economy. Even if tariffs are replaced with trade deals, the fear that they will stick has thrown supply chains in disarray, drawn forward some purchases, postponed others, while leaving consumers and business owners in a general state of discontent. The first indication that horrible sentiment is starting to impact activity was seen in last week’s gasoline usage numbers, which showed a year-over-year decline of 2.8% in the four-week moving average. This is a real-time pulse on consumer discretionary spending.

Additionally, the longer it takes for risk asset prices to recover, the more likely a negative wealth effect could take hold, as the top 10% of households by income own the majority of stock market wealth and account for 50% of overall consumer spending. Yesterday, the S&P 500 formed a discouraging “death cross,” whereby the 50-day moving average fell below the 200-day moving average. It is not as ominous as it seems, though, despite suggesting a loss of near-term price momentum because the index has been higher one year later in 80% of the prior 20 occurrences. I think it will be higher than yesterday’s close of 5,596 one year from now as well.

I think the seesaw analogy is appropriate because markets will ultimately force the president to back down from the tariff policies that very few support in the days and weeks ahead. The loss of wealth from declining stock prices, higher borrowing costs from lower bond prices, and the increasing probability of a painful recession will only serve to weaken his base of support.

If that process starts sooner rather than later, investors can start focusing on multiple tailwinds, the most important of which is monetary policy easing by the Fed to loosen financial conditions. The consensus is now expecting four more rate cuts from the central bank this year, lowering the benchmark rate from its current 4.25-4.5% range to 3.25-3.5%. I still see three rate cuts, as I have since the beginning of this year, because I think the Fed will still be dealing with the unknown impact of a more modest version of the current tariff program. Regardless, this is a tailwind for risk asset prices during the second half of this year.

data.title: ASML: €570/Share Is As Cheap As It Gets--I'm Buying Q1 Earnings Dip
data.description:
Tariff-related uncertainty is not only making stock-picking incredibly hard, with stocks trading on politics and news instead of fundamentals, but it's clouding the outlook for European crown jewel ASML Holding (NASDAQ:ASML).

ASML reported its Q1 earnings this morning, and Amsterdam-domiciled stock has fallen 5% as the company reported solid earnings but once again missed order expectations as US-induced import tariffs are clouding the demand for chip-making equipment.

While AI-related demand, particularly for its high-end EUV chip-making machines, was resilient, ASML has seen its DUV sales weakening as demand for smartphones, appliances, and electronics faced a period of consolidation, alongside chip export restrictions to China.

While I previously argued tariff may actually be a tailwind for ASML's product line as more production is moved to the US, increasing demand for its equipment, the short-term uncertainty with some customers lowering their CAPEX is prompting the company to be cautious, now guiding to land 2025 at the lower end of its guidance.

Previous Coverage

Two months ago, I wrote an article titled "ASML: The Turnaround Is Now Confirmed," rating the stock "Strong Buy" amidst strong AI-related demand, recovery in sight for the lower-end of chip-making, and forward-twelve-month valuation at 25x its earnings.

Since then, the stock has pulled back 5%, yet the performance of the broader market (SP500) has been worse, pulling as much as 10%. Semiconductor stocks are inherently volatile, and it's encouraging to see that ASML's volatility is, in fact, lower than that of the US stock market, with participants starting to understand how cheap, in fact, ASML's shares are. If you are a US-based investor and buy EU-domiciled or ADR shares, you will also benefit from the strengthening EUR/USD.

In my last coverage, I expected US/Dutch government export restrictions to have a minimal impact on ASML's demand. New fabs planned in the US and Europe would offset the decline in China-driven sales despite the delays in fab development due to a limited skilled workforce and Intel (INTC) challenges. Don't forget, ASML can recognize its revenue only once its equipment is delivered to the customer.

ASML's management was cautiously optimistic, as weak guidance pointed to sufficient chip-making capacity, but recovery in consumer spending, particularly in PC and smartphone sales, would likely result in a new round of CAPEX investment cycle, with recovery in H2 2025 - H1 2026

Today, we have new clouds on the horizon, and it's time to provide an update to my investment thesis.

Q1 Earnings

ASML's Q1 earnings were solid yet missed the estimates on the top line and beat on the bottom.

ASML reported Net Sales of €7.74B, matching the company's previous guidance, but analysts were expecting €7.8B amidst the increased AI-related spending.

The Gross margin, a metric I watch closely to see if ASML retains its monopoly-like status with industry-leading pricing power, landed at 54%, above the company's guidance, the highest in the last 5 quarters, helped by a favorable product mix with 56% EUV sales, compared to 42% in Q4 2024.

As a result, the bottom line, net income, landed at €2.36B or €6.00 EPS, above analyst expectations of €2.3B.

In Q1, ASML shipped its first High NA system, the next generation of extreme ultraviolet lithography designed for cutting-end chip-making. The average sticker price is €400M per unit, double the cost of previous low-NA EUV equipment.

Q1 saw ASML selling 77 lithography systems, with an average price of €100M, significantly fewer systems compared to Q2 sales of 132 systems, but at a lower price of €70M per unit.

The product mix is heavily influenced by geographical exposure, as you can see below on the right, as each market is purchasing different systems influenced by the export restrictions.

You might be asking yourself, if earnings were solid, why is the stock dropping 5%?

The answer is net bookings.

ASML's net bookings—a key KPI to track order demand—in Q1 came at €3.94B, falling short of analysts' estimates of €4.89B, with ASML's CEO confirming the challenges but still expecting 2025-2026 to be growth years:

"Our conversations so far with customers support our expectation that 2025 and 2026 will be growth years. However, the recent tariff announcements have increased uncertainty in the macro environment and the situation will remain dynamic for a while. As previously shared, artificial intelligence continues to be the primary growth driver in our industry. It has created a shift in the market dynamics that benefits some customers more than others, contributing to both upside potential and downside risks as reflected in our 2025 revenue range.

Net Bookings are a very volatile indicator of ASML's workload, as you can judge for yourself from the graph below (the blue columns). Given the current backlog worth €35B, ASML's business remains safe and sound. Each quarter, the business fulfills around €6-8B of its backlog, and ASML is fully booked for another five quarters.

The focus of investors here is on the potential damage tariffs may inflict on ASML. The temporary weakness that we are seeing is, in my opinion, not driven by the lack of demand for ASML's machines but by the uncertainty, with businesses like Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF), Intel pausing their CAPEX spending, seeking more clarity on how the back-and-forth import duties will play out and if indeed relocating production to the US is the only viable option.

Initially, Trump announced a 32% tariff on Taiwanese goods, where most advanced chips are produced, just to be revised to 10% for the next 90 days.

ASML's revenue exposure to the US in Q1 was 16% of sales and in Q4 2024 28%, but I expect that with the new fabs coming online, more of ASML's equipment will be heading to the US, and a 10% tariff won't damage their demand.

Let's be honest: whether with a 10% or 25% tariff, a cutting-edge chipmaker won't refuse the best-in-class technology if there is no other viable alternative.

ASML's guidance now points to net sales of €7.2-7.7B in Q2, with the gross margin expected to continue trending above 50%.

The management also doubled down on their long-term guidance, expecting full-year 2025 sales to land on the lower end of the €30-40B range, marking 10-15% growth from full-year 2024 sales of €28.2B.

As European-based companies don't pay a regular, fixed quarterly dividend, ASML now intends to pay a full-year 2024 dividend of €6.4/share. Considering the three previous dividends of €1.52 paid in 2024, the final dividend in 2025 will be €1.84 if approved during the Annual General Meeting.

Previously, I criticized ASML for its inefficient buyback strategy. The business didn't buy back any of its shares during the weakness we saw in Q4 2024. Finally, we can see that the company repurchased €2.7B worth of shares under the 2022-2025 buyback program.

Yet, given the weakness, with shares now trading in deep value territory at ~570EUR/share, I would appreciate a more aggressive buyback strategy.

Valuation & Forward Growth

ASML's shares are currently valued at a Blended P/E of 31.7x, below their 15Y normalized P/E of 33.2.

If we, for instance, consider the FY25 forecasted EPS of $25.38, the shares are on a 9-month forward basis, trading at ~27x forward P/E.

I'll let you make up your own mind, but for me, a business that is projected to grow its EPS at a rate of 20-30% annually for the next few years, this seems far too cheap.

We don't speak about "any" business but monopoly-like players with cutting-edge technology.

Yes, ASML's business is highly cyclical, even though Installed Base Management, that are less cyclical are increasly contributing more to the overall sales, selling a low volume, 50-150 pieces of equipment per quarter, is seen as risk.

FactSet, is now forecasting the following EPS growth, which is actuallyan improvement from my last coverage:

  • FY2025: EPS of $25.38E, 27% YoY growth
  • FY2026: EPS of $30.66E, 21% YoY growth
  • FY2027: EPS of $36.53E, 19% YoY growth

I see ASML's valuation as a rare opportunity to buy the best-in-class lithography equipment maker. Myself, I am collecting the low-hanging fruit, buying more shares below €600/share, setting the stage for future outperformance as the secular tailwinds of increased digitalization are unlikely to fade.

In my view, investors could earn up to 20-25% annual ROR if we have more clarity on the tariff-related uncertainty and the sentiment improves, pushing the stock towards its 15Y average P/E of 33.2x.

Investor's Takeaway

ASML's Q1 earnings sent the stock lower by 5%, even though the results were surprisingly good.

The issue is that ASML's stock is not trading based on its reported earnings but based on its net bookings and the outlook, which is currently overshadowed by the US-induced import tariffs, with key customers delaying their CAPEX spending, seeking more clarity.

If tariffs stay, cutting-edge chip manufacturers are unlikely not to purchase ASML's chipmaking equipment because of a 10-25% tariff, but consumer demand could be impacted.

ASML's management doubled down on its Q2 and full-year 2025 guidance despite the challenges, and I see the shares as undervalued, increasing my exposure at ~570EUR/share.
